era_context: The Hellenistic world in the immediate aftermath of Alexander the Great's death (323 BCE). His vast empire is fragmenting among competing diadochi (successors). In Greece, the Lamian War has recently concluded (322 BCE), reasserting Macedonian control. In Egypt, Ptolemy I is consolidating power and has secured Alexander's body. In Asia, Seleucus and Antigonus are maneuvering for territorial dominance. The Indian subcontinent is experiencing the rise of the Mauryan Empire under Chandragupta. China is in the late Warring States period.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Detailed records of Central Asian kingdoms

events.title: Seleucus I gains control of Babylon
events.description: Seleucus I Nicator, one of Alexander's generals, consolidates his position as satrap of Babylon after the partition of Triparadisus (321 BCE). This marks the beginning of Seleucid dynastic control over Mesopotamia and the eastern portions of Alexander's empire. Seleucus's control of Babylon gives him access to vast resources and positions him as a major power broker among the diadochi. This event is foundational to the eventual Seleucid Empire, which will dominate the Near East for nearly two centuries.
key_figures: Seleucus I Nicator (Macedonian general, future founder of Seleucid dynasty)

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica (Library of History), Book 18-19

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of Seleucus's power in Babylon occurred over 321–320 BCE. Sources are not contemporary but derive from Hellenistic historians writing within a few generations. The exact date of 320 is conventional; sources suggest the process was gradual.
cross_references: Leads to 312-BCE Battle of Gaza and Seleucus's further expansion

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya consolidates northern India
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya, founder of the Mauryan Empire, is in the process of consolidating control over the Gangetic plain and northern India. By 320 BCE, he has defeated the Nanda dynasty and is establishing the administrative and military infrastructure of what will become the largest empire in pre-Islamic India. The Mauryan Empire will eventually extend from the Indus to the Bay of Bengal. Chandragupta's rise represents a major shift in Indian political organization, moving toward centralized imperial rule.
events.certainty_note: The dating of Chandragupta's reign is debated. Traditional Indian chronology places his rule c. 322–298 BCE; some scholars argue for 315–290 BCE. The year 320 is used here as a conventional midpoint of his consolidation phase. No contemporary written sources from India survive; dating relies on later chronicles and Greek accounts.
cross_references: Contemporary with Seleucus I's rise in Mesopotamia
Leads to 305-BCE diplomatic contact between Seleucus and Chandragupta

events.title: Ptolemy I secures Egypt and Alexander's body
events.description: Ptolemy I Soter consolidates his control over Egypt following the Lamian War (322 BCE). He has secured Alexander the Great's body (brought to Egypt c. 321 BCE) and is establishing Alexandria as the capital and intellectual center of his domain. Ptolemy's control of Egypt, with its vast agricultural wealth and strategic position, makes him one of the most powerful diadochi. The establishment of the Ptolemaic dynasty will make Egypt a major Hellenistic power for the next three centuries.
key_figures: Ptolemy I Soter (Macedonian general, founder of Ptolemaic dynasty)
Alexander the Great (deceased, but his body is a symbol of legitimacy)
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy's control of Egypt by 320 BCE is well-attested in later Greek sources. The transfer of Alexander's body to Egypt is dated to c. 321 BCE by most sources. Papyrological evidence confirms Ptolemaic administrative structures in place by this period.
cross_references: Leads to ongoing conflicts with other diadochi, especially Antigonus

events.title: Antigonus I consolidates Phrygia and central Anatolia
events.description: Antigonus I Monophthalmus (the One-Eyed), another of Alexander's generals, is consolidating control over Phrygia and central Anatolia. By 320 BCE, he is emerging as a major power broker and will soon challenge Seleucus and Ptolemy for dominance. Antigonus's control of Anatolia gives him significant military and economic resources. His ambitions will lead to the Wars of the Diadochi, which will dominate the next two decades.

sources.name: Plutarch, Life of Demetrius (son of Antigonus)

events.certainty_note: Antigonus's consolidation of Anatolia occurred gradually over 321–320 BCE and beyond. Sources are not contemporary but derive from Hellenistic historians. The exact boundaries and extent of his control in 320 are not precisely documented.
cross_references: Leads to 315-BCE conflicts with Seleucus and Ptolemy

events.title: Lysimachus establishes control in Thrace
events.description: Lysimachus, another of Alexander's generals, consolidates his position as satrap of Thrace. By 320 BCE, he is establishing himself as a major regional power. Lysimachus will eventually become one of the most powerful diadochi and will found cities, including Lysimachia. His control of Thrace gives him access to the Hellespont and significant military resources.
events.certainty_note: Lysimachus's control of Thrace by 320 BCE is attested in later sources, but the exact date of consolidation is not precisely documented. Sources suggest his position was secure by this time.
cross_references: Leads to conflicts with Antigonus and Seleucus

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of the diadochi presents them as clearly defined power blocs by 320 BCE, but modern scholarship emphasizes the fluidity and uncertainty of this period. Boundaries were not fixed, alliances shifted rapidly, and the 'partition of Triparadisus' (321 BCE) was not a definitive settlement but rather a temporary arrangement. Some scholars argue that Seleucus's position in Babylon was less secure in 320 than later sources suggest, and that his rise to prominence was more gradual and contested than the traditional account implies. Additionally, the significance attributed to individual diadochi in 320 may be retrospectively inflated based on their later prominence; at the time, the outcome of the succession struggle was far from certain.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 320 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Diodorus Siculus and later Hellenistic historians. These sources are not contemporary and were written generations after the events. There is a strong bias toward the Macedonian/Greek perspective; the viewpoints of conquered peoples and non-Greek civilizations are largely absent. For India, sources are even more problematic: no contemporary Indian written sources survive, and dating relies on later Buddhist and Jain texts, which are themselves uncertain. Chinese sources for this period are more reliable but focus on internal Chinese affairs. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Archaeological evidence is sparse for this specific year but becomes more abundant for the Hellenistic period as a whole.

graph_edges.note: Seleucus's control of Babylon sets the stage for his conflict with Antigonus and Demetrius
Chandragupta's consolidation of India leads to diplomatic contact with Seleucus
Ptolemy's secure position in Egypt allows him to participate in the coalition against Antigonus
Seleucus and Antigonus are both consolidating power in this period, setting up future conflicts
